title: Planche: Six Months In
description:
The planche is not a trick. It's a full-body isometric battle between your anterior deltoids, serratus anterior, and gravity — and gravity has been winning for most of my life.

I started with tuck holds at 10-second sets. Couldn't get past 15 seconds without shaking. The key unlock for me was understanding scapular protraction — you're not just holding yourself up, you're actively pushing the floor away and rounding your upper back into the movement.

After three months I hit advanced tuck. After five, straddle planche for 4 seconds. Not pretty, but it's there. The wrist conditioning alone took six weeks of daily prep work before I could even attempt longer sessions.

title: First Handstand Pull-Up
description:
Handstand pull-ups require overhead pressing strength, scapular stability, and a freestanding handstand that doesn't wobble — all three at once. For a long time I had the pulling strength but zero overhead stability.

The fix was training handstands against a wall first, building straight-body compression, then moving to freestanding holds of 30+ seconds before attempting any pulling movement. The pull itself is counterintuitive — you lead with elbows flaring out wide before pulling them down and in.

First clean rep happened on a random Tuesday. Didn't even film it. Did it again immediately three times just to make sure it was real.

title: Front Lever: Hitting Full for the First Time
description:
The front lever gets overlooked compared to planche because it looks easier. It is not easier. It demands extraordinary lats, rear delts, and core stiffness — and if your thoracic spine rounds even slightly, you drop.

I spent four months on tuck variations, then straddle, then one-leg extensions before I could hold a full lever for 2 seconds. My breakthrough was adding weighted pull-ups (20kg) as a strength supplement, which gave me the lat thickness to actually maintain body position.

Current best is a 5-second hold, filmed and verified. The feeling of the full lock is unlike anything else in calisthenics — completely horizontal, no assistance, just tension.

title: Strict Muscle-Up: Why Form Matters
description:
Anyone can kip a muscle-up. The strict version is a different exercise entirely — it forces you to generate enough pulling power to transition cleanly over the bar without momentum.

I spent six weeks unable to break through the transition. The issue: I was pulling to my chest instead of pulling to my hips. Once you understand the muscle-up as a hip-height pull followed by a push, the pattern clicks.

Now sitting at 5 strict reps. The foundation this built for ring work and bar muscle-up variations is enormous. Everything in upper-body calisthenics flows through this movement.
